/** * A record batch is a container for records in the Kafka log format. * *

In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1), a batch consisted of a single record * if no compression was enabled, but could contain many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic * versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records regardless of compression.

* *

Thread Safety: Implementations of this interface are not thread-safe. External * synchronization is required for concurrent access.

* *

Role in Kafka Protocol: Record batches are fundamental to the Kafka wire protocol and * the commit log storage format. They provide efficient serialization of multiple records with * shared metadata (producer ID, epoch, timestamps, compression). The batch format enables features * like idempotent producers, transactional support, and efficient log compaction.

* *

Performance Characteristics: For compressed batches, decompression is lazy—records are * decompressed on-demand via iterators. For uncompressed batches, record data is directly accessible * from the backing buffer without additional copying.

* * @see DefaultRecordBatch DefaultRecordBatch for magic v2+ implementation * @see MemoryRecords MemoryRecords for in-memory batch collections * @see Record Individual record in a batch */ public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable {

 *  BaseOffset => Int64
 *  Length => Int32
 *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32
 *  Magic => Int8
 *  CRC => Uint32 (CRC-32C from attributes to end of batch)
 *  Attributes => Int16
 *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32
 *  BaseTimestamp => Int64 (timestamp of first record, or delete horizon if flag set)
 *  MaxTimestamp => Int64
 *  ProducerId => Int64 (or -1 if not set)
 *  ProducerEpoch => Int16
 *  BaseSequence => Int32
 *  RecordsCount => Int32
 *  Records => [Record]

* When compression is enabled, compressed record data follows directly after the count field.

* *

Thread Safety: This class is not thread-safe. Batch state can be modified in-place * (e.g., via {@link #setMaxTimestamp(TimestampType, long)}) and must be externally synchronized for * concurrent access.

* *

CRC Computation: The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial covers all bytes from the attributes * field through the end of the batch. * *

Log Compaction Semantics: Magic v2+ preserves the first and last offset/sequence numbers * from the original batch during log cleaning. This ensures producer state can be correctly restored * after leader failover. Base sequence is needed for duplicate detection. Empty batches may be retained * to preserve producer state until a new sequence is written or the producer ID expires.

* *

Performance Characteristics: *

    *
  • Header fields (offsets, timestamps, producer ID) are efficiently accessible directly from the * buffer without iteration.
    • *

    • *

/**
 * RecordBatch implementation for magic 2 and above. The schema is given below:
 *
 * RecordBatch =>
 *  BaseOffset => Int64
 *  Length => Int32
 *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32
 *  Magic => Int8
 *  CRC => Uint32
 *  Attributes => Int16
 *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32 // also serves as LastSequenceDelta
 *  BaseTimestamp => Int64
 *  MaxTimestamp => Int64
 *  ProducerId => Int64
 *  ProducerEpoch => Int16
 *  BaseSequence => Int32
 *  RecordsCount => Int32
 *  Records => [Record]
 *
 * Note that when compression is enabled (see attributes below), the compressed record data is serialized
 * directly following the count of the number of records.
 *
 * The CRC covers the data from the attributes to the end of the batch (i.e. all the bytes that follow the CRC). It is
 * located after the magic byte, which means that clients must parse the magic byte before deciding how to interpret
 * the bytes between the batch length and the magic byte. The partition leader epoch field is not included in the CRC
 * computation to avoid the need to recompute the CRC when this field is assigned for every batch that is received by
 * the broker. The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial is used for the computation.
 *
 * On Compaction: Unlike the older message formats, magic v2 and above preserves the first and last offset/sequence
 * numbers from the original batch when the log is cleaned. This is required in order to be able to restore the
 * producer's state when the log is reloaded. If we did not retain the last sequence number, then following
 * a partition leader failure, once the new leader has rebuilt the producer state from the log, the next sequence
 * expected number would no longer be in sync with what was written by the client. This would cause an
 * unexpected OutOfOrderSequence error, which is typically fatal. The base sequence number must be preserved for
 * duplicate checking: the broker checks incoming Produce requests for duplicates by verifying that the first and
 * last sequence numbers of the incoming batch match the last from that producer.
 *
 * Note that if all of the records in a batch are removed during compaction, the broker may still retain an empty
 * batch header in order to preserve the producer sequence information as described above. These empty batches
 * are retained only until either a new sequence number is written by the corresponding producer or the producerId
 * is expired from lack of activity.
 *
 * There is no similar need to preserve the timestamp from the original batch after compaction. The BaseTimestamp
 * field therefore reflects the timestamp of the first record in the batch in most cases. If the batch is empty, the
 * BaseTimestamp will be set to -1 (NO_TIMESTAMP). If the delete horizon flag is set to 1, the BaseTimestamp
 * will be set to the time at which tombstone records and aborted transaction markers in the batch should be removed.
 *
 * Similarly, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the maximum timestamp of the current records if the timestamp type
 * is CREATE_TIME. For LOG_APPEND_TIME, on the other hand, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the timestamp set
 * by the broker and is preserved after compaction. Additionally, the MaxTimestamp of an empty batch always retains
 * the previous value prior to becoming empty.
 *
 * The current attributes are given below:
 *
 *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  | Unused (7-15) | Delete Horizon Flag (6) | Control (5) | Transactional (4) | Timestamp Type (3) | Compression Type (0-2) |
 *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 */
public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRecordBatch {

# Kafka Record Batch Serialization - Javadoc Summary

This document summarizes the Javadoc additions made to Kafka's record batch serialization classes in `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/`.

## Overview

Three core classes handle record batch serialization in Kafka:
1. **RecordBatch** - Interface defining batch operations
2. **DefaultRecordBatch** - Concrete implementation for magic v2+
3. **MemoryRecords** - In-memory collection and management of batches

## Class-Level Documentation Enhancements

### RecordBatch (Interface)

**Purpose:** Container and interface for Kafka message batches across all format versions.

**Key Additions:**
- **Thread Safety:** Explicitly documented as NOT thread-safe (requires external synchronization)
- **Role in Protocol:** Explained fundamental importance in wire protocol and commit log format
- **Performance Characteristics:**
  - Lazy decompression for compressed batches (on-demand via iterators)
  - Direct buffer access for uncompressed data (no copying)
  - Enables idempotent producers, transactions, and log compaction features

**Cross-References:**
- {@link DefaultRecordBatch} for magic v2+ implementation
- {@link MemoryRecords} for batch collections
- {@link Record} for individual records

### DefaultRecordBatch (Concrete Implementation)

**Purpose:** Binary serialization and parsing of record batches in Kafka's v2+ format.

**Key Additions:**
- **Thread Safety:** NOT thread-safe; state can be modified in-place (e.g., CRC updates)
- **Binary Format:** Documented full schema with field offsets and sizes
- **CRC Computation:**
  - Uses CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial
  - Covers attributes field through end of batch
  - Partition leader epoch excluded to avoid recomputation
- **Log Compaction Semantics:** Explained preservation of first/last offset/sequence across compaction
- **Performance Characteristics:**
  - Header fields efficiently accessible without iteration
  - Compressed batches use lazy decompression
  - Record count efficiently retrievable via countOrNull()

**Key Methods Enhanced:**
- `baseTimestamp()` - Explains role in timestamp delta calculation and delete horizon
- `recordInputStream()` - Documents buffer requirements for decompression
- `streamingIterator()` - Performance impact of buffer allocation and reuse

### MemoryRecords (In-Memory Collection)

**Purpose:** ByteBuffer-backed collection and filtering of record batches.

**Key Additions:**
- **Thread Safety:** NOT thread-safe; backing buffer modified in-place, requires synchronization
- **Use Cases:** Reading/modifying log buffers, network data, batch collections
- **Performance Characteristics:**
  - Lazy batch iteration (parsed incrementally)
  - Lazy decompression (on-demand during record read)
  - No copying of record data; direct buffer slicing
  - Caching of validBytes() after first computation
- **Buffer States:** Documents read-only vs. writable modes

**Key Methods Enhanced:**
- `readableRecords()` - Factory method for reading existing batches
- `validBytes()` - Explains partial batch handling and caching behavior
- `filterTo()` - Documents log compaction filtering, batch/record retention logic
- `withRecords()` - Convenience factory for creating simple non-idempotent batches
- `builder()` - Creates builders for constructing new batches

## Method-Level Documentation

### Critical Method: RecordBatch.streamingIterator()

Enhanced with performance guidance:
- **Memory Efficiency:** Delays decompression until records requested
- **Buffer Allocation:** Notes that small batches allocate large buffers (64 KB for LZ4)
- **Optimization:** Recommends reusable BufferSupplier for small batch scenarios
- **Resource Management:** Emphasizes need to close iterator for cleanup

### Critical Method: MemoryRecords.filterTo()

Enhanced for log compaction use case:
- **Filtering Process:** Step-by-step explanation of batch and record evaluation
- **Retention Modes:** DELETE, RETAIN_EMPTY, DELETE_EMPTY semantics
- **Performance:** Buffer overflow handling, decompression on-demand
- **Metrics:** Explains returned FilterResult structure (bytes read/retained, max offset/timestamp)

### Critical Method: MemoryRecords.validBytes()

Enhanced to clarify edge cases:
- **Partial Batches:** Explains why result may be < sizeInBytes()
- **Caching:** Performance note about first-call iteration cost
- **Use Cases:** When to use vs. sizeInBytes()

## Documentation Best Practices Applied

### Thread Safety Documentation
Every class explicitly states thread-safety guarantee:
- Format: "Thread Safety: This class is **not thread-safe**" with explanation
- Synchronization required at what boundary
- Example: batch mutation during log appending

### Performance Implications
Performance characteristics documented for methods that could be optimized:
- Lazy decompression and iteration patterns
- Buffer allocation costs for decompression
- Caching behavior and amortization
- Recommendations for BufferSupplier usage

### Kafka Protocol Context
Documentation explains why each class exists in protocol architecture:
- Producer ID/epoch for idempotence
- Sequence numbers for duplicate detection
- CRC-32C for corruption detection
- BaseOffset/sequence preservation during compaction

### Cross-References
Javadoc uses `{@link}` tags to connect related classes:
- RecordBatch → DefaultRecordBatch (implementation)
- MemoryRecords → MemoryRecordsBuilder (creation)
- DefaultRecordBatch → Record (composition)
- filterTo() → RecordFilter (dependency)

1. **RecordBatch.java** (Interface)
   - Enhanced class-level Javadoc
   - Improved method documentation:
     - isValid() / ensureValid() - CRC explanation
     - baseOffset() - Performance implications
     - streamingIterator() - Buffer management details

2. **DefaultRecordBatch.java** (Implementation)
   - Comprehensive class-level documentation with schema, thread-safety, CRC, compaction
   - Enhanced method documentation:
     - baseTimestamp() - Timestamp delta and delete horizon explanation
     - recordInputStream() - Decompression buffer guidance

3. **MemoryRecords.java** (Collection)
   - Detailed class-level documentation with use cases, thread-safety, performance
   - Enhanced factory methods:
     - readableRecords() - Purpose and buffer lifetime
     - builder() - Configuration defaults and use cases
     - withRecords() - Convenience factory documentation
   - Enhanced instance methods:
     - validBytes() - Partial batch semantics and caching
     - filterTo() - Log compaction process and metrics

✓ **No Logic Changes:** Implementation unchanged; documentation-only additions
✓ **Cross-References:** Related classes and methods linked via {@link}

## Key Takeaways for Users

### For Log Reading
- Use `MemoryRecords.readableRecords()` to wrap buffers for batch iteration
- Use `streamingIterator()` with reusable BufferSupplier for memory efficiency
- Thread-safety requires external synchronization

### For Record Production
- Use `MemoryRecordsBuilder` or `MemoryRecords.withRecords()` factory methods
- For idempotent producers, use `withIdempotentRecords()` (includes sequence numbers)
- For transactional producers, use `withTransactionalRecords()`

### For Log Compaction
- `filterTo()` implements retention logic; pass custom RecordFilter
- Understand that producer state (base sequence) is preserved in empty batches
- Performance optimized through lazy decompression

### For Correctness
- BaseOffset/LastOffset preserved during compaction
- BaseSequence/LastSequence preserved for duplicate detection
- BaseTimestamp preserved (or set to delete horizon) for tombstone expiration
- CRC-32C protects batch integrity (partition leader epoch excluded)

## Implementation Architecture Notes

The three classes work together in the Kafka protocol:

```
Wire Protocol / Log Bytes
         ↓
MemoryRecords.readableRecords(ByteBuffer)
         ↓
Iterator<MutableRecordBatch> via batches()
         ↓
DefaultRecordBatch (parses v2+ format)
         ↓
Iterator<Record> via streamingIterator()
         ↓
Individual Record processing
```

During write:
```
Producer Records
         ↓
MemoryRecordsBuilder (creates batches)
         ↓
DefaultRecordBatch.writeEmptyHeader() / append()
         ↓
MemoryRecords.build() returns ByteBuffer
         ↓
Wire Protocol / Log Bytes
```

During compaction:
```
MemoryRecords.filterTo(RecordFilter, ...)
         ↓
RecordFilter.checkBatchRetention() & shouldRetainRecord()
         ↓
Filtered batches/records written back
         ↓
Producer state (base sequence) preserved in empty batches
```

/**
 * RecordBatch implementation for magic 2 and above. The schema is given below:
 *
 * RecordBatch =>
 *  BaseOffset => Int64
 *  Length => Int32
 *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32
 *  Magic => Int8
 *  CRC => Uint32
 *  Attributes => Int16
 *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32 // also serves as LastSequenceDelta
 *  BaseTimestamp => Int64
 *  MaxTimestamp => Int64
 *  ProducerId => Int64
 *  ProducerEpoch => Int16
 *  BaseSequence => Int32
 *  RecordsCount => Int32
 *  Records => [Record]
 *
 * Note that when compression is enabled (see attributes below), the compressed record data is serialized
 * directly following the count of the number of records.
 *
 * The CRC covers the data from the attributes to the end of the batch (i.e. all the bytes that follow the CRC). It is
 * located after the magic byte, which means that clients must parse the magic byte before deciding how to interpret
 * the bytes between the batch length and the magic byte. The partition leader epoch field is not included in the CRC
 * computation to avoid the need to recompute the CRC when this field is assigned for every batch that is received by
 * the broker. The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial is used for the computation.
 *
 * On Compaction: Unlike the older message formats, magic v2 and above preserves the first and last offset/sequence
 * numbers from the original batch when the log is cleaned. This is required in order to be able to restore the
 * producer's state when the log is reloaded. If we did not retain the last sequence number, then following
 * a partition leader failure, once the new leader has rebuilt the producer state from the log, the next sequence
 * expected number would no longer be in sync with what was written by the client. This would cause an
 * unexpected OutOfOrderSequence error, which is typically fatal. The base sequence number must be preserved for
 * duplicate checking: the broker checks incoming Produce requests for duplicates by verifying that the first and
 * last sequence numbers of the incoming batch match the last from that producer.
 *
 * Note that if all of the records in a batch are removed during compaction, the broker may still retain an empty
 * batch header in order to preserve the producer sequence information as described above. These empty batches
 * are retained only until either a new sequence number is written by the corresponding producer or the producerId
 * is expired from lack of activity.
 *
 * There is no similar need to preserve the timestamp from the original batch after compaction. The BaseTimestamp
 * field therefore reflects the timestamp of the first record in the batch in most cases. If the batch is empty, the
 * BaseTimestamp will be set to -1 (NO_TIMESTAMP). If the delete horizon flag is set to 1, the BaseTimestamp
 * will be set to the time at which tombstone records and aborted transaction markers in the batch should be removed.
 *
 * Similarly, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the maximum timestamp of the current records if the timestamp type
 * is CREATE_TIME. For LOG_APPEND_TIME, on the other hand, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the timestamp set
 * by the broker and is preserved after compaction. Additionally, the MaxTimestamp of an empty batch always retains
 * the previous value prior to becoming empty.
 *
 * The current attributes are given below:
 *
 *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  | Unused (7-15) | Delete Horizon Flag (6) | Control (5) | Transactional (4) | Timestamp Type (3) | Compression Type (0-2) |
 *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 */
public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRecordBatch {

  • For empty batches (after compaction), this remains the timestamp of the original first * record.

  • If the delete horizon flag is set, this represents the time at which tombstone records * and aborted transaction markers should be removed.

  2. If batch retention is {@link RecordFilter.BatchRetention#DELETE}, the entire batch is skipped.

  3. Otherwise, each record is evaluated via {@link RecordFilter#shouldRetainRecord(RecordBatch, Record)}.
    4. *
  4. Retained records are written to {@code destinationBuffer}, possibly in a new batch if * modification was necessary.
    5. *

* *

Performance: For compressed batches, this method decompresses records on-demand using * the supplied {@code decompressionBufferSupplier}. Reusable buffer suppliers significantly improve * performance for small batches where allocation dominates. Buffer overflow is handled transparently * by allocating larger buffers as needed.

If overflow occurs, a larger buffer * is allocated and returned in {@code FilterResult#outputBuffer()} * @param decompressionBufferSupplier supplier for decompression buffers.     public FilterResult filterTo(RecordFilter filter, ByteBuffer destinationBuffer, BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier) {

arguments.content:
# Kafka Record Batch Serialization - Javadoc Summary

This document summarizes the Javadoc additions made to Kafka's record batch serialization classes in `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/`.

## Overview

Three core classes handle record batch serialization in Kafka:
1. **RecordBatch** - Interface defining batch operations
2. **DefaultRecordBatch** - Concrete implementation for magic v2+
3. **MemoryRecords** - In-memory collection and management of batches

## Class-Level Documentation Enhancements

### RecordBatch (Interface)

**Purpose:** Container and interface for Kafka message batches across all format versions.

**Key Additions:**
- **Thread Safety:** Explicitly documented as NOT thread-safe (requires external synchronization)
- **Role in Protocol:** Explained fundamental importance in wire protocol and commit log format
- **Performance Characteristics:**
  - Lazy decompression for compressed batches (on-demand via iterators)
  - Direct buffer access for uncompressed data (no copying)
  - Enables idempotent producers, transactions, and log compaction features

**Cross-References:**
- {@link DefaultRecordBatch} for magic v2+ implementation
- {@link MemoryRecords} for batch collections
- {@link Record} for individual records

### DefaultRecordBatch (Concrete Implementation)

**Purpose:** Binary serialization and parsing of record batches in Kafka's v2+ format.

**Key Additions:**
- **Thread Safety:** NOT thread-safe; state can be modified in-place (e.g., CRC updates)
- **Binary Format:** Documented full schema with field offsets and sizes
- **CRC Computation:**
  - Uses CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial
  - Covers attributes field through end of batch
  - Partition leader epoch excluded to avoid recomputation
- **Log Compaction Semantics:** Explained preservation of first/last offset/sequence across compaction
- **Performance Characteristics:**
  - Header fields efficiently accessible without iteration
  - Compressed batches use lazy decompression
  - Record count efficiently retrievable via countOrNull()

**Key Methods Enhanced:**
- `baseTimestamp()` - Explains role in timestamp delta calculation and delete horizon
- `recordInputStream()` - Documents buffer requirements for decompression
- `streamingIterator()` - Performance impact of buffer allocation and reuse

### MemoryRecords (In-Memory Collection)

**Purpose:** ByteBuffer-backed collection and filtering of record batches.

**Key Additions:**
- **Thread Safety:** NOT thread-safe; backing buffer modified in-place, requires synchronization
- **Use Cases:** Reading/modifying log buffers, network data, batch collections
- **Performance Characteristics:**
  - Lazy batch iteration (parsed incrementally)
  - Lazy decompression (on-demand during record read)
  - No copying of record data; direct buffer slicing
  - Caching of validBytes() after first computation
- **Buffer States:** Documents read-only vs. writable modes

**Key Methods Enhanced:**
- `readableRecords()` - Factory method for reading existing batches
- `validBytes()` - Explains partial batch handling and caching behavior
- `filterTo()` - Documents log compaction filtering, batch/record retention logic
- `withRecords()` - Convenience factory for creating simple non-idempotent batches
- `builder()` - Creates builders for constructing new batches

## Method-Level Documentation

### Critical Method: RecordBatch.streamingIterator()

Enhanced with performance guidance:
- **Memory Efficiency:** Delays decompression until records requested
- **Buffer Allocation:** Notes that small batches allocate large buffers (64 KB for LZ4)
- **Optimization:** Recommends reusable BufferSupplier for small batch scenarios
- **Resource Management:** Emphasizes need to close iterator for cleanup

### Critical Method: MemoryRecords.filterTo()

Enhanced for log compaction use case:
- **Filtering Process:** Step-by-step explanation of batch and record evaluation
- **Retention Modes:** DELETE, RETAIN_EMPTY, DELETE_EMPTY semantics
- **Performance:** Buffer overflow handling, decompression on-demand
- **Metrics:** Explains returned FilterResult structure (bytes read/retained, max offset/timestamp)

### Critical Method: MemoryRecords.validBytes()

Enhanced to clarify edge cases:
- **Partial Batches:** Explains why result may be < sizeInBytes()
- **Caching:** Performance note about first-call iteration cost
- **Use Cases:** When to use vs. sizeInBytes()

## Documentation Best Practices Applied

### Thread Safety Documentation
Every class explicitly states thread-safety guarantee:
- Format: "Thread Safety: This class is **not thread-safe**" with explanation
- Synchronization required at what boundary
- Example: batch mutation during log appending

### Performance Implications
Performance characteristics documented for methods that could be optimized:
- Lazy decompression and iteration patterns
- Buffer allocation costs for decompression
- Caching behavior and amortization
- Recommendations for BufferSupplier usage

### Kafka Protocol Context
Documentation explains why each class exists in protocol architecture:
- Producer ID/epoch for idempotence
- Sequence numbers for duplicate detection
- CRC-32C for corruption detection
- BaseOffset/sequence preservation during compaction

### Cross-References
Javadoc uses `{@link}` tags to connect related classes:
- RecordBatch → DefaultRecordBatch (implementation)
- MemoryRecords → MemoryRecordsBuilder (creation)
- DefaultRecordBatch → Record (composition)
- filterTo() → RecordFilter (dependency)

## Files Modified

1. **RecordBatch.java** (Interface)
   - Enhanced class-level Javadoc
   - Improved method documentation:
     - isValid() / ensureValid() - CRC explanation
     - baseOffset() - Performance implications
     - streamingIterator() - Buffer management details

2. **DefaultRecordBatch.java** (Implementation)
   - Comprehensive class-level documentation with schema, thread-safety, CRC, compaction
   - Enhanced method documentation:
     - baseTimestamp() - Timestamp delta and delete horizon explanation
     - recordInputStream() - Decompression buffer guidance

3. **MemoryRecords.java** (Collection)
   - Detailed class-level documentation with use cases, thread-safety, performance
   - Enhanced factory methods:
     - readableRecords() - Purpose and buffer lifetime
     - builder() - Configuration defaults and use cases
     - withRecords() - Convenience factory documentation
   - Enhanced instance methods:
     - validBytes() - Partial batch semantics and caching
     - filterTo() - Log compaction process and metrics

## Compliance with Quality Standards

✓ **Thread-Safety:** Every class documents thread-safety guarantee explicitly
✓ **Performance:** Serialization/deserialization characteristics noted with optimization guidance
✓ **No Private Methods:** Documentation only added to public API
✓ **Standard Tags:** Uses @param, @return, @throws, @see, {@link}
✓ **No Logic Changes:** Implementation unchanged; documentation-only additions
✓ **Cross-References:** Related classes and methods linked via {@link}

## Key Takeaways for Users

### For Log Reading
- Use `MemoryRecords.readableRecords()` to wrap buffers for batch iteration
- Use `streamingIterator()` with reusable BufferSupplier for memory efficiency
- Thread-safety requires external synchronization

### For Record Production
- Use `MemoryRecordsBuilder` or `MemoryRecords.withRecords()` factory methods
- For idempotent producers, use `withIdempotentRecords()` (includes sequence numbers)
- For transactional producers, use `withTransactionalRecords()`

### For Log Compaction
- `filterTo()` implements retention logic; pass custom RecordFilter
- Understand that producer state (base sequence) is preserved in empty batches
- Performance optimized through lazy decompression

### For Correctness
- BaseOffset/LastOffset preserved during compaction
- BaseSequence/LastSequence preserved for duplicate detection
- BaseTimestamp preserved (or set to delete horizon) for tombstone expiration
- CRC-32C protects batch integrity (partition leader epoch excluded)

## Implementation Architecture Notes

The three classes work together in the Kafka protocol:

```
Wire Protocol / Log Bytes
         ↓
MemoryRecords.readableRecords(ByteBuffer)
         ↓
Iterator<MutableRecordBatch> via batches()
         ↓
DefaultRecordBatch (parses v2+ format)
         ↓
Iterator<Record> via streamingIterator()
         ↓
Individual Record processing
```

During write:
```
Producer Records
         ↓
MemoryRecordsBuilder (creates batches)
         ↓
DefaultRecordBatch.writeEmptyHeader() / append()
         ↓
MemoryRecords.build() returns ByteBuffer
         ↓
Wire Protocol / Log Bytes
```

During compaction:
```
MemoryRecords.filterTo(RecordFilter, ...)
         ↓
RecordFilter.checkBatchRetention() & shouldRetainRecord()
         ↓
Filtered batches/records written back
         ↓
Producer state (base sequence) preserved in empty batches
```
